summary: The dataset 'q1' contains 10 problems (q1 through q10), each with exactly 100 open issues, indicating uniformly high and unresolved activity across the board. The flat distribution provides no clear signal of which problems have more traction, and no proof-status metadata distinguishes solvable problems from stalled ones. Additional granularity on issue types and resolution progress is needed before reliable prioritization is possible.
problems: All 10 problems (q1-q10) carry 100 open issues each, with zero recorded as resolved or in progress.
Uniform issue counts make it impossible to distinguish high-traction problems from neglected ones using current data.
No proof-status detail is available beyond raw issue counts, limiting assessment of solvability.
Potential data artifact: identical 100-issue counts may indicate a capped/truncated metric or import error rather than true activity.
No metadata on issue categories (e.g., definitional, computational, verification) prevents pattern analysis of common blockers.
highlights: Comprehensive coverage: every problem in the dataset is being actively tracked with issues logged.
Consistent baseline across all 10 problems enables fair comparative analysis once richer metadata is added.
High issue volume suggests strong engagement and thorough scrutiny of each problem.

suggested_todos.title: Verify whether the uniform 100-issue count is a real metric or a data capping/import artifact
Enrich dataset with proof-status fields (open/in-progress/resolved) to enable solvability assessment
Categorize open issues by type to surface common patterns and recurring blockers
Add per-problem activity timestamps to identify which problems have recent momentum
Run a manual spot-review of 2-3 problems to ground-truth the issue data quality
Define triage criteria to rank problems once differentiating metadata is available
